content.text: Load testing an agentic AI application is qualitatively different from load testing a conventional API. LLM API calls are slow (1–30 seconds), expensive (metered per token), and subject to provider rate limits that interact with application-level concurrency. A naive load test that ignores these properties will either exhaust the API budget in minutes, trigger rate limiting cascades, or produce results that do not reflect real user behaviour. Effective load testing of LLM API integration paths requires a purpose-built approach to scenario design, concurrency modeling, and metric collection.
What it is

Load testing LLM API integration paths means exercising the end-to-end request path of an agentic application — from the load generator through the API gateway, agent server, LLM API, and downstream tool services — under controlled, increasing traffic to measure latency, throughput, error rates, and resource utilisation. The term integration path refers to the complete chain of API calls made for a single user request: one or more LLM calls, zero or more tool calls, and the final response assembly.

Unlike load testing a simple CRUD API (where each request takes milliseconds and the system under test is fully owned), LLM load testing must account for the external LLM provider as both a dependency and a constraint. The provider's rate limits, concurrent request caps, and token throughput limits define the ceiling of what the application can achieve, independent of internal architectural choices. A load test must model these limits realistically.

Load tests for LLM paths are typically run in two modes: against a real LLM API (to measure actual end-to-end latency including token generation time) and against a mock LLM API (to isolate the application's own infrastructure bottlenecks and measure capacity at rates the real API would not permit or afford).
Why it matters

LLM-backed applications fail under load in ways that conventional applications do not. The most common failure modes are: silent rate limit cascades (the application hits the provider's requests-per-minute limit, starts queuing requests, and user-facing latency rises from 3 seconds to 45 seconds without any error), token-per-minute exhaustion (the application processes requests correctly at low concurrency but generates too many tokens per second at high concurrency, causing 429 errors that manifest as agent workflow failures), and connection pool exhaustion (the HTTP connection pool to the LLM API is sized for low-concurrency usage and becomes the bottleneck at production traffic levels).

Load testing surfaces these failure modes before they affect real users. Without explicit load testing, rate limit behaviour and connection pool sizing are typically discovered in production during traffic spikes — the worst possible time.
Benefits
Identifies the rate limit threshold before it manifests as a production incident.
Quantifies the p95 and p99 latency the application delivers under realistic concurrent user counts.
Reveals connection pool sizing problems before the first production traffic spike.
Provides a performance baseline against which future code and configuration changes can be regressed.
Enables cost modeling: the load test data translates directly into projected token spend at production traffic levels.
Informs horizontal scaling decisions: mock-LLM load tests show how many application server replicas are needed to serve a target RPS.
Trade-offs
Real-API load tests are expensive: running 100 concurrent users for 10 minutes against GPT-4o can cost hundreds of dollars.
Mock-LLM load tests measure application infrastructure, not real LLM latency; results do not directly predict user-facing performance.
LLM request latency has high variance (p99 can be 3-5x p50); small load test sample sizes produce unstable percentile estimates.
Provider rate limits vary by API key tier and can change without notice; load test results can become stale if provider limits change.
Think time modeling for agentic workflows is complex: users do not issue sequential requests at a fixed rate; they wait for the agent to complete before issuing the next request.
How it works
Scenario design

A load test scenario models a realistic user session: the user sends a message, waits for the agent to complete (which may take several turns and multiple tool calls), then sends the next message after a configured think time. The concurrency model for LLM applications is typically closed (a fixed number of virtual users, each completing one request before starting the next) rather than open (a fixed request rate). This reflects real user behaviour: users wait for responses.
Rate limit modeling

The load test must track the application's token consumption per minute and compare it against the provider's TPM (tokens per minute) limit. When the test is designed to approach the TPM limit, the scenario must include realistic input token counts (prompt size) and expected output token counts (completion length). A scenario that uses short prompts will underestimate TPM consumption relative to production.
Mock LLM for infrastructure testing

A mock LLM server responds to chat completion requests with pre-generated responses of a configured size after a configured delay. This allows the load test to run at arbitrary concurrency and RPS without incurring token costs or hitting provider rate limits. The mock reveals how the application server, connection pools, database, and caching layer perform independently of LLM latency.
Metrics collection

In addition to standard HTTP metrics (latency, RPS, error rate), LLM load tests collect LLM-specific metrics: time to first token (TTFT) for streaming responses, total token count per request, number of LLM calls per user request (turns), and rate limit hit frequency. These metrics are reported at p50/p95/p99 percentiles over the test duration.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Load testing architecture for LLM API integration paths showing load generator, API gateway, LLM API (real or mock), and tool services

content.text: The diagram shows the full integration path targeted by the load test. The load generator (Locust or k6) sends virtual user sessions through the API gateway, agent server, LLM API, and tool services. Key metrics are captured at each stage. The LLM API can be the real provider endpoint or a mock server for infrastructure-only testing.
When to use it
Run a load test before the first production deployment of any agentic application to establish a performance baseline.
Run mock-LLM load tests when evaluating infrastructure changes: connection pool sizing, caching, replica count.
Run real-API load tests when evaluating the actual user experience at production concurrency levels (budget permitting).
Run load tests after significant prompt changes that alter average token counts per request.
Do not run load tests against production environments without rate limiting the test to below 10% of production capacity.
How to implement

# locustfile.py — load test for agentic AI application
import time
import json
import random
from locust import HttpUser, task, between, events
from locust.env import Environment

# Representative user queries that approximate production token counts
QUERIES = [
    "Summarize the key findings of the attached quarterly report.",
    "What are the main differences between BERT and GPT architectures?",
    "Draft a reply to the following customer complaint email.",
    "List the top 5 risks in the project plan and suggest mitigations.",
]

class AgentUser(HttpUser):
    # Think time between completing one request and starting the next
    wait_time = between(10, 30)  # seconds; models reading the response
    
    def on_start(self):
        """Authenticate once per virtual user session."""
        resp = self.client.post("/auth/token", json={
            "username": f"loadtest-user-{self.environment.runner.user_count}",
            "password": "loadtest-secret",
        })
        self.token = resp.json()["access_token"]
        self.headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {self.token}"}
    
    @task
    def chat_with_agent(self):
        query = random.choice(QUERIES)
        
        start = time.monotonic()
        with self.client.post(
            "/api/v1/chat",
            json={"message": query, "session_id": f"load-test-{id(self)}"},
            headers=self.headers,
            catch_response=True,
            stream=True,
        ) as response:
            if response.status_code == 429:
                response.failure(f"Rate limited: {response.text}")
                return
            
            first_chunk_received = False
            total_chars = 0
            for chunk in response.iter_lines():
                if chunk and not first_chunk_received:
                    ttft = time.monotonic() - start
                    # Report TTFT as a custom metric
                    events.request.fire(
                        request_type="LLM_TTFT",
                        name="time_to_first_token",
                        response_time=ttft * 1000,
                        response_length=0,
                        exception=None,
                        context={},
                    )
                    first_chunk_received = True
                total_chars += len(chunk)
            
            if response.status_code != 200:
                response.failure(f"HTTP {response.status_code}")
            else:
                response.success()

# mock_llm_server.py — lightweight mock LLM API for infrastructure testing
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
import asyncio
import json
import time
import random

app = FastAPI()

MOCK_COMPLETIONS = [
    "Based on the provided context, the key finding is that revenue grew 12% year-over-year.",
    "The primary distinction between BERT and GPT lies in their training objectives: BERT uses masked language modeling while GPT uses causal language modeling.",
    "Thank you for bringing this to our attention. We sincerely apologize for the inconvenience caused and will resolve this within 24 hours.",
    "The top 5 risks identified are: timeline slippage, budget overrun, resource availability, technical debt, and regulatory compliance.",
]

@app.post("/v1/chat/completions")
async def mock_chat(request: Request):
    body = await request.json()
    stream = body.get("stream", False)
    
    # Simulate token generation latency (configurable via environment)
    base_delay = float(os.getenv("MOCK_LLM_BASE_DELAY", "1.5"))
    jitter = random.uniform(0, 0.5)
    total_delay = base_delay + jitter
    
    completion = random.choice(MOCK_COMPLETIONS)
    
    if stream:
        async def generate():
            await asyncio.sleep(total_delay * 0.3)  # TTFT delay
            words = completion.split()
            for i, word in enumerate(words):
                chunk = {
                    "id": "mock-123",
                    "object": "chat.completion.chunk",
                    "choices": [{"delta": {"content": word + " "}, "index": 0}]
                }
                yield f"data: {json.dumps(chunk)}\n\n"
                await asyncio.sleep(total_delay * 0.7 / len(words))
            yield "data: [DONE]\n\n"
        return StreamingResponse(generate(), media_type="text/event-stream")
    else:
        await asyncio.sleep(total_delay)
        return {
            "id": "mock-123",
            "object": "chat.completion",
            "choices": [{"message": {"role": "assistant", "content": completion}, "index": 0, "finish_reason": "stop"}],
            "usage": {"prompt_tokens": 150, "completion_tokens": len(completion.split()), "total_tokens": 150 + len(completion.split())}
        }
Examples

#!/bin/bash
# Run load test: ramp from 1 to 50 users over 5 minutes, hold for 10 minutes
locust \
  --headless \
  --host http://staging-agent-api.internal \
  --users 50 \
  --spawn-rate 2 \
  --run-time 15m \
  --html load-test-report.html \
  --csv load-test-results \
  --only-summary \
  -f locustfile.py

# Check p95 latency is under SLA (10 seconds)
python3 -c "
import csv
with open('load-test-results_stats.csv') as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    for row in reader:
        if row['Name'] == '/api/v1/chat':
            p95 = float(row['95%'])
            print(f'p95 latency: {p95:.0f}ms')
            assert p95 < 10000, f'SLA BREACH: p95 {p95}ms exceeds 10000ms'
            break
print('SLA check passed.')
"
Real-world example

A legal document analysis platform uses GPT-4o to process uploaded documents. A load test revealed that at 20 concurrent users, the application regularly hit the API's requests-per-minute limit (RPM = 60 for the team's tier), causing 429 errors that were surfaced to users as internal server errors. The load test identified three improvements: upgrading to a higher RPM tier, adding an exponential backoff retry handler with jitter in the LLM client, and introducing a request queue that limited concurrent in-flight LLM calls to 50 to stay within the rate limit.

After these changes, a second load test at 30 concurrent users showed zero 429 errors, p95 latency of 8.2 seconds (within the 10-second SLA), and a 95th-percentile token throughput of 1,800 tokens per second — well within the provider's TPM limit. The team saved the real-API load test results as a CI baseline artifact and re-ran the mock-LLM load test on every PR that touched the HTTP client or connection pool configuration.
Common mistakes
Using open load (fixed RPS) instead of closed load (fixed virtual users) — open load does not model the closed-loop nature of agent interactions where users wait for responses.
Not including think time between requests — generates artificial concurrency levels that don't reflect real user behaviour.
Running real-API load tests without a spending cap — a misconfigured test that runs for an hour can consume thousands of dollars of API budget.
Not measuring TTFT for streaming responses — total latency misses the user-perceived responsiveness for streaming agents.
Ignoring 429 rate limit errors in load test results — treating them as non-critical hides the true capacity ceiling.
Running load tests only on a local laptop — network latency from the load generator to the API gateway must match production conditions.
Best practices
Always run mock-LLM load tests before real-API load tests to identify infrastructure bottlenecks without token cost.
Set a hard spending cap on the API key used for load testing — a $50-100 cap prevents runaway test costs.
Run load tests from the same network region as the production deployment to get accurate network latency.
Collect p50, p95, and p99 latency percentiles — p50 alone hides tail latency issues.
Include at least 300 requests per test scenario for stable p99 estimates.
Report rate limit hits (429 count, 429 rate) as first-class metrics alongside latency and error rate.
Automate mock-LLM load tests in CI as a performance regression gate triggered on every PR touching the HTTP client or connection pool.
Performance & scalability

The achievable RPS for an LLM-backed application is fundamentally constrained by the provider's rate limits. At a typical mid-tier API limit of 3,500 RPM (≈58 RPS) and an average LLM call duration of 3 seconds, the application can sustain at most 58 × 3 = 174 concurrent in-flight LLM requests before rate limiting kicks in. For multi-turn agents that make three LLM calls per user request, this translates to roughly 58 concurrent user sessions at the limit.

Horizontal scaling of the application server layer beyond this ceiling does not increase throughput — it only adds redundancy and reduces individual server resource pressure. True throughput scaling requires either a higher rate-limit tier from the provider or routing requests across multiple API keys (which requires careful design to avoid violating provider terms of service).
Tooling & ecosystem
Locust
: Python-based load testing framework with excellent support for custom metrics and streaming response testing.
k6
: JavaScript/TypeScript load testing tool with excellent streaming support and built-in cloud execution.
Artillery
: YAML-driven load testing framework; good for HTTP scenarios without custom code.
Gatling
: Scala/JVM load testing framework with detailed HTML reports; strong for high-concurrency scenarios.
OpenAI Rate Limit Monitor
: unofficial tool that tracks token and request consumption in real time during load tests.
Prometheus + Grafana
: standard stack for visualizing load test metrics and storing them for cross-run comparison.

Security & reliability

Load tests must use dedicated test API keys that are isolated from production keys. A load test that exhausts a production API key's rate limit will degrade real user traffic. Create a separate API key for load testing with a spending cap and a lower rate limit tier; this ensures the load test can never exhaust production capacity even if misconfigured.

Load test results (especially those that include request/response bodies) may contain sensitive information. Load test logs and artifacts must be stored in access-controlled locations and purged after the retention period. Never include real user data in load test scenarios; use synthetic data that approximates production token counts without exposing PII.
Key takeaways
LLM load testing requires a closed-load model (virtual users with think time) to accurately reflect agent interaction patterns.
Run mock-LLM load tests for infrastructure capacity before real-API load tests to avoid token costs during bottleneck discovery.
Rate limit hits (429 errors) are first-class failure metrics in LLM load tests — not secondary to latency.
TTFT (time to first token) is the primary user-perceived latency metric for streaming agents; total latency is secondary.
The achievable concurrent user count is fundamentally bounded by the provider's RPM and TPM limits, not by application server capacity.
Set hard spending caps on load test API keys to prevent runaway token costs from misconfigured tests.
Automate mock-LLM load tests in CI on every change to HTTP client configuration, connection pool settings, or rate limit retry logic.
